scriptId: cs_v3_ACCUMULATION_L40_Excessive phone or social media use
historyId: h_v3_ACCUMULATION_L40_Excessive phone or social media use

goal: cut down on excessive phone and social media use
Prefer small, sustainable changes over an all-or-nothing detox.
Reduce mindless scrolling through quick, low-friction hacks.
Cap social app time to about 90 minutes daily.
Reduce weekday screen time from ~4 hours to a lower target.
constraints: Maintain a non-judgmental, supportive tone in all guidance.
Two-week trial period to assess changes and adjust.
Only use built-in phone tools (Screen Time/Digital Wellbeing); no third-party monitoring apps.
Core work hours require being reachable for calls and texts; cannot fully disconnect during those hours.
Cap social app time to ~90 minutes daily using built-in tools.
Weekday screen time averages ~4 hours; aim to reduce.
Do not delete apps; keep apps for coordination with friends and volunteer groups.
decisions: Adopt a mixed framework: environmental tweaks with friction, Habit substitution, and selective Strict limits.
Emphasize low-friction, sustainable options over drastic measures.
Prioritize low-effort, quick-win strategies for busy schedules.
Adopt ultra-low-friction tracking with 1–3 metrics and brief daily check-ins.
Adopt a non-judgmental, supportive guidance style.
Incorporate gamified elements (streaks, points, time-bank, progress visuals, micro-celebrations) to support habit formation while keeping it low-effort.
Define core, always-on rules (e.g., 30–60 minute phone-free buffer before bed; turn off badges/notifications overnight; concise morning check-in with DND).
Allow weekend-friendly flexibility with opt-in rules (longer social time, planned long scroll, higher total screen-time targets by a small percentage).
Establish an exception plan for events and travel as planned deviations.
Track weekday and weekend metrics separately for 1–2 weeks to evaluate spillover and adjust rules.
Use only built-in tracking tools for the two-week trial (Screen Time/Digital Wellbeing) and no third-party trackers.
Focus on top social apps for targeted friction (short-video/ photo feeds) and implement app-level limits and friction steps.
entities: Habit substitution
Strict limits
Environmental tweaks
Behavioral design / friction
Notification triage
Do Not Disturb windows
Home-screen minimalism
Micro-substitutes
Grayscale or icon removal
Turn off nonessential notifications and badge icons
Move distracting apps off the home screen (second page or inside a folder)
Reduce autoplay for videos in apps and browsers
Replace app shortcuts with website bookmarks for apps you don't need often
Hide or remove the app from the dock so it's not always visible
Disable lock-screen previews for social apps
Limit push refresh: turn off background app refresh for social apps
Set a one-minute pause rule: wait 60 seconds and a quick physical action
Put your charger or phone in a designated spot away from your main work area or bedside
Use built-in app timers (e.g., 30–45 min per app per day)
Turn on reduce motion and smaller text animations where available
Enable reader mode or text-only view for news sites
Effectiveness of built-in time limits and notifications
Common bypass methods for built-in limits
Strategies to strengthen built-in limits (add friction)
Morning routines: condensed check window, phone-out-of-bed, habit stack
Evening routines: phone-free buffer, night rules, short wind-down ritual
App placement and home screen tweaks: minimal home screen, move social apps to folder/second page, remove from dock, replace with bookmarks
Notification rules: allow only critical alerts, disable social push and badges, disable lock-screen previews, email push rules

Daily template

Endless refresh loops/infinite scroll triggers
Nightly tracking entry format (Date | Total screen time | Social time | Pickups | Morning rule Y/N | Urge 1–5 | Points)
Weekly review template (Win / Problem / One tweak for next week)
Selective in-app muting for coordination apps
Mute nonessential group chats while keeping notifications for important conversations
Disable in-app autoplay for videos in coordination apps
Move feed-heavy apps off home screen or into folders
Disable Background App Refresh for coordination apps
Use browser version for casual browsing while keeping native app for messaging
Structured check-ins: schedule short sessions to reduce spontaneous browsing
Reachable Focus/Do Not Disturb with exceptions for urgent coordination
Ringtone or vibration rules for priority coordination contacts/groups
Check group updates during scheduled social coordination sessions
open: Provide weekend schedule details (e.g., social events, childcare, sleep changes) to tailor split rules.
Ask user to specify the top 1–2 social apps to target for limits and friction.
Clarify triggers: stress or waiting that lead to scrolling and endless-refresh feeds.
